versionNotes: Sprint 4 co-design — students help shape Sprint 4 through 4 aspects with individual reflection + partner discussion
title: Sprint 4 Co-Design
description: Help design Sprint 4. For each of 4 aspects, reflect individually, then discuss with a partner. Switch partners after Aspect 2.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 1: What Should We Be Able to Do?

Think beyond specific assignments. After 16 weeks in this course, what should every student be able to do or demonstrate about professional development and self-directed learning? Think about capabilities that would matter to an employer, to your career, to your ability to grow independently.

Instructions: Write your individual response first. You will share and discuss with a partner next.
questions.prompt: What should every student who completes this course be able to do or demonstrate? Name 2-3 specific capabilities — not topics covered, but things you can actually DO.
questions.placeholder: Example: Every student should be able to (1) honestly assess where they are in a skill and create a concrete plan to improve — not just say 'I need to get better at networking' but identify specific actions like 'I will attend one meetup and follow up with two people,' (2) learn something independently without waiting for an assignment to tell them what to study, like how I taught myself Docker during Sprint 2 because my triad partner showed me it was a gap, and (3) give useful feedback to peers that actually helps them improve, not just 'good job.'

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 1

Turn to the person next to you. Share your response. Listen to theirs. Then discuss together:

  • Where do your answers overlap? Where do they differ?
  • Think about the entire class — would your answers serve everyone, or just people like you?
  • What capability would you add after hearing your partner?
questions.prompt: After discussing with your partner, write down: (1) your partner's name, (2) the capabilities you both agreed matter most, and (3) anything that changed in your thinking.
questions.placeholder: Example: Partner name: Kyle. We both agreed that self-assessment accuracy is critical — Kyle pointed out that in Sprint 1 most of us rated ourselves higher than we actually were, and the revision assignment proved it. He added something I hadn't considered: the ability to ask for help effectively. Not just 'I'm stuck' but being able to articulate exactly what you've tried and where you're blocked. That changed my thinking because I realized I've been asking vague questions and getting vague answers.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 2: What Activities Help Us Get There?

Given what you said we should learn in Aspect 1 — what types of activities would actually develop those abilities? Think about what has worked so far: self-assessments, triad accountability, information diet audits, reflections. What has not worked? Consider both in-class sessions and homework.

Instructions: Write your individual response. Same partner discussion follows.
questions.prompt: What types of activities — in class and as homework — would help develop the capabilities you identified? Name at least one activity type that has worked well this semester and one that has not.
questions.placeholder: Example: The triad accountability check-ins actually worked because having two other people expecting me to report progress made me follow through. The information diet audit was eye-opening — I didn't realize how much low-quality content I was consuming until I tracked it. What hasn't worked: some of the self-assessment scales feel abstract. Rating myself 1-5 on 'metacognition' doesn't mean much. What would be better: concrete scenarios like 'describe a time this week you caught yourself avoiding a hard task' — that's assessable.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 2

Share your response. Then discuss:

  • Which activities from Sprints 1-3 actually changed how you think or work?
  • Which felt like busywork?
  • If you could design one in-class activity and one homework activity for Sprint 4, what would they be?
questions.prompt: After discussing: (1) your partner's name (same partner), (2) activities you agreed work best, (3) what you would change or add for Sprint 4.
questions.placeholder: Example: Partner name: Kyle. We agreed the triad check-ins and the networking event were the most valuable — the check-ins because they create real accountability, and the networking event because talking to professionals made the skills feel relevant, not academic. For Sprint 4, we'd change the reflection format: instead of weekly open-ended reflections, do a 'weekly delta' — just answer 'what's different about how I think or work compared to last week?' One question, specific answer. We'd also add a peer mock-defense where triads practice presenting their portfolios to each other before the real thing.

questions.infoBlock:
Switch partners. Find a different person to sit with for Aspects 3 and 4.

Aspect 3: How Do We Know We Got There?

Sprint 4 ends with a demo and a portfolio defense where you prove your growth across the UMPIRE cycle. How should we assess whether you have actually developed the capabilities from Aspect 1? What counts as evidence? What format lets you show your growth most honestly?

Failing and learning from it IS the learning. A defense that shows you tried something, it broke, and you understood why — that is not lesser evidence of growth. It IS the evidence. The student who shows a polished result they never struggled with has less to defend than the student who shows what went wrong and what they learned from it.
questions.prompt: How should we assess your learning — what counts as real evidence of growth? And what defense format would let you show that growth most effectively? Consider: presentations, portfolio walkthroughs, peer challenges, Q&A sessions, or something else.
questions.placeholder: Example: Real evidence isn't a perfect self-assessment score — it's being able to point to a specific moment where my understanding changed. Like in Sprint 2, I thought I was good at giving feedback until the peer evaluation exercise showed me I was only giving surface-level comments. The evidence is my Sprint 3 feedback compared to Sprint 2 — you can see the difference in specificity. For the defense, I think a 1-on-1 portfolio walkthrough works best because it's hard to fake understanding in a live conversation. The instructor can ask follow-up questions that a slide deck can't answer.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 3

Share with your NEW partner. Discuss:

  • What kind of evidence would convince a skeptic that you actually grew?
  • Should the defense be 1-on-1 with the instructor, in small groups presenting each other's work, or something else?
  • Think about what would work for the whole class, not just you.

Remember: Evidence of struggle and recovery IS evidence of learning. When discussing what counts as proof of growth, don't dismiss the failures — they are the strongest proof you have.
questions.prompt: After discussing with your NEW partner: (1) new partner's name, (2) what evidence format you agreed on, (3) what defense structure would work best for the class.
questions.placeholder: Example: New partner name: Annabelle. We agreed that a 'growth portfolio' with side-by-side comparisons works best — show your Week 2 self-assessment next to your Week 14 version and explain what changed and why. Annabelle made a great point: some people learn a lot but aren't great presenters, so the defense should be conversational, not performative. We think 1-on-1 with the instructor, 10-15 minutes, where you walk through your portfolio and answer questions. The instructor's questions are the real test — if you understand your own growth, you can answer anything they ask.

questions.infoBlock:
Aspect 4: What Should Sprint 4 Look Like?

Sprint 4 is about preparing for and delivering your portfolio defense. The defense covers your entire journey across the UMPIRE cycle. Given everything you discussed in Aspects 1-3, what should Sprint 4 include to best prepare you?
questions.prompt: What should Sprint 4 look like? What weekly structure would help you prepare for the portfolio defense? What would make the final 4 weeks feel like they matter?
questions.placeholder: Example: Sprint 4 should have a clear weekly rhythm: Week 13 — finalize your portfolio artifacts and get triad feedback. Week 14 — mock defense with your triad where they play 'skeptical instructor.' Week 15 — revise based on mock feedback and do a final self-assessment comparison. Week 16 — actual defense. What would make this meaningful: if the defense questions are genuinely unpredictable. Not 'tell me about Sprint 2' but 'show me evidence that you actually changed how you approach X.' That would make people prepare honestly instead of rehearsing a script.

questions.infoBlock:
Partner Discussion — Aspect 4

Share with same partner as Aspects 3-4. Discuss:

  • If you designed Sprint 4 for the whole class, what would the non-negotiables be?
  • What should be flexible?
  • One concrete recommendation to Prof. Sathya.
questions.prompt: Final discussion capture: (1) partner's name, (2) your group's top recommendation for Sprint 4, (3) one thing that should definitely stay and one thing that should definitely change.
questions.placeholder: Example: Partner name: Annabelle. Our top recommendation: add a mandatory mock defense in Week 14 where triads practice presenting to each other and give honest feedback. This gives everyone a chance to see what works before the real thing. Keep: the triad accountability structure — it's the single thing that made Sprint 2 different from Sprint 1 for most people. Change: reduce the number of separate reflection submissions and replace them with one cumulative 'sprint journal' that you update weekly. Fewer submissions, same depth, less busywork feeling.
